entries.population: LBCL relapsing after CD19 CAR-T therapy.
entries.finding: For disease relapsing after CD19 CAR-T, allogeneic transplant can produce durable remissions but requires attaining a chemosensitive complete response first, so it is reasonably held in reserve for the post-CAR-T setting rather than used upfront.
entries.limits: Review/retrospective; no randomized comparison; selection-biased populations.

entries.population: Older adults (>=65) receiving systemic cancer therapy.
entries.finding: The guideline recommends a geriatric assessment across function, comorbidity, cognition, nutrition, and psychosocial domains for older adults starting systemic therapy, because these domains predict toxicity and mortality independently of performance status. It grounds using multi-domain assessment rather than ECOG alone.
entries.limits: Guidance, not disease-specific efficacy data.

entries.population: Older adults receiving systemic cancer therapy.
entries.finding: The 2023 update reaffirms routine geriatric assessment to guide treatment-intensity decisions in older adults, with evidence that assessment-driven care reduces toxicity. It supports the field-level rule that a single performance-status score is insufficient in this population.
entries.limits: Guidance, not disease-specific.

entries.population: Adults with aggressive B-cell lymphoma refractory to or relapsed within 12 months of first-line therapy.
entries.finding: Second-line tisagenlecleucel did NOT improve event-free survival versus standard salvage care in early-failure aggressive B-cell lymphoma; the trial was negative. This is why tisagenlecleucel is not a second-line option, in contrast to axi-cel and liso-cel.
entries.anchor_quote: no event-free survival benefit vs standard care
entries.limits: Negative trial; longer time-to-infusion and bridging design differed from ZUMA-7/TRANSFORM. Does not address tisa-cel in later lines.

entries.population: Patients undergoing apheresis for CD19 CAR-T after prior bendamustine.
entries.finding: Recent bendamustine exposure before apheresis was associated with impaired T-cell fitness and worse CAR-T outcomes. This grounds the rule against giving bendamustine-containing regimens (e.g., pola-BR) when cellular therapy is still intended.
entries.limits: Retrospective; association not proven causation; effect strongest with recent/heavy bendamustine exposure.

entries.population: Secondary CNS involvement by B-cell lymphoma treated with CD19 CAR-T.
entries.finding: Pooled non-randomized data suggest CD19 CAR-T is feasible with durable responses in a subset of patients with secondary CNS lymphoma and without prohibitive neurotoxicity, supporting cellular therapy after CNS disease is controlled. Lower-ICANS products (liso-cel, tisa-cel) are generally preferred when CNS disease is present.
entries.limits: Meta-analysis of non-randomized series/case reports; no prospective trial dedicated to secondary CNS lymphoma.

entries.population: Selected older adults (>=70/>=75) receiving CD19 CAR-T.
entries.finding: Registry and real-world series show CD19 CAR-T is feasible with comparable efficacy in selected older patients, with age- and frailty-associated increases in neurotoxicity and heavy logistical requirements (apheresis, manufacturing lead time, sustained proximity to a certified center, and a reliable caregiver). This grounds treating CAR-T eligibility as a fitness-plus-logistics composite rather than a numeric age cutoff.
entries.anchor_quote: no label-based upper age limit for CAR-T
entries.limits: Retrospective, selection-biased toward fitter older patients; logistics and caregiver requirements are real gatekeepers.

entries.population: Pharmacology of CNS-directed lymphoma therapy.
entries.finding: High-dose methotrexate, high-dose cytarabine, and thiotepa achieve cytotoxic concentrations in CSF and brain parenchyma, whereas standard R-CHOP-backbone drugs do not; intrathecal drug penetrates only millimeters from the CSF surface. This grounds why R-DHAP (cytarabine-containing) is more CNS-active than R-ICE and why parenchymal disease needs systemic CNS-penetrant therapy, not intrathecal alone.
entries.limits: Pharmacology review (primary-CNS-lymphoma framed); supports drug selection, not a specific regimen comparison.

entries.population: Regulatory/market status of copanlisib (Aliqopa).
entries.finding: Copanlisib was voluntarily withdrawn from the US market in 2023 after its confirmatory trial failed, and it has limited/uncertain CNS penetration. It is therefore not a valid current bridging option, especially for CNS-predominant disease.
entries.limits: Regulatory notice, not a clinical study; documents unavailability and weak CNS rationale.

entries.population: Older cancer patients with good performance status (ECOG 0-1).
entries.finding: A substantial share of older patients who look fit by ECOG performance status have deficits on geriatric assessment (in function, falls, cognition, or polypharmacy). This documents that ECOG alone systematically under-detects vulnerability and justifies requiring geriatric-domain corroboration.
entries.limits: Observational; establishes discordance, not a treatment effect.

entries.population: R/R DLBCL; phase 3 of epcoritamab monotherapy vs investigator-choice chemotherapy.
entries.finding: Topline results reported by press release describe improved progression-free survival, complete response, and duration of response for epcoritamab monotherapy versus investigator-choice chemotherapy, but overall survival did not reach statistical significance. No peer-reviewed publication is available yet.
entries.limits: Press release only, no peer-reviewed data; overall survival not statistically significant; render as 'grounded, press-release only'.

entries.population: R/R DLBCL after >=2 prior lines (3L+).
entries.finding: Single-agent subcutaneous epcoritamab produced high response rates in R/R DLBCL after two or more prior lines, and is the basis for its regulatory approval as monotherapy at 3rd line and beyond. It defines the on-label setting against which any 2L or combination use is off-label.
entries.limits: Single-arm; on-label indication is monotherapy at 3L+ only; does not establish 2L or combination use.

entries.population: ASCT-ineligible R/R DLBCL; single-arm bispecific-plus-chemotherapy combination (epcoritamab + GemOx).
entries.finding: Epcoritamab plus GemOx produced deep, durable responses (ORR 85%, CR 61%; median duration of CR ~24 months) in a real-world-like transplant-ineligible R/R DLBCL population with median age 72. Because only epcoritamab MONOTHERAPY is approved (and only at 3rd line or later), the GemOx combination and any 2L use are off-label but prospectively supported.
entries.limits: Single-arm, no comparator; the combination and 2L use are off-label (approval is monotherapy, 3L+); enrolled during COVID-19 with related deaths.

entries.population: Older adults (>=65) with DLBCL, prospectively assessed.
entries.finding: A simplified geriatric assessment integrating age, activities of daily living, instrumental ADLs, and comorbidity classified older DLBCL patients as fit, unfit, or frail, and this classification predicted survival and tolerability better than chronologic age. Fit older patients tolerated fuller-intensity therapy; frailty, not age, drove outcomes.
entries.anchor_quote: frailty, not chronologic age, predicts survival
entries.limits: Developed in the first-line setting; a validated stratifier, not a treatment trial.

entries.population: R/R DLBCL after >=2 prior lines (3L+).
entries.finding: Fixed-duration single-agent glofitamab produced durable complete responses in heavily pretreated R/R DLBCL and is the basis for its US approval as monotherapy at 3rd line and beyond. Ramp-up (step-up) dosing with obinutuzumab pretreatment is required to mitigate cytokine release syndrome.
entries.limits: Single-arm; on-label at 3L+ monotherapy only; CRS concentrated in cycle-1 step-up requiring monitored administration.

entries.population: Classification of mature lymphoid neoplasms.
entries.finding: The consensus classification likewise defines the high-grade double-hit entity by MYC and BCL2 rearrangements, reinforcing that a MYC FISH method (rearrangement vs amplification) must be known before labeling a case double-hit.
entries.limits: Classification/consensus, not outcome data.

entries.population: ASCT-INELIGIBLE relapsed/refractory DLBCL after 1-3 prior lines; PRIMARY-REFRACTORY patients were EXCLUDED.
entries.finding: Tafasitamab plus lenalidomide showed durable responses (ORR ~58%, CR ~41%) in autologous-transplant-ineligible R/R DLBCL, but the trial explicitly excluded primary-refractory disease and enrolled a lower-tumor-burden population. There is therefore no efficacy evidence for tafasitamab-lenalidomide in primary-refractory disease, which is why it is excluded there.
entries.limits: Single-arm; excluded primary-refractory and rapidly progressive/bulky disease; not a head-to-head comparison.

entries.population: R/R DLBCL after >=2 prior lines (3L+).
entries.finding: Loncastuximab tesirine, a CD19-directed antibody-drug conjugate, produced responses in heavily pretreated R/R DLBCL and is approved from the third line. As a CD19-targeted agent it raises antigen-loss considerations if used before a planned CD19 CAR-T.
entries.limits: Single-arm; 3L+ indication; CD19-directed, relevant to sequencing before CD19 CAR-T.

entries.population: Secondary CNS lymphoma (systemic aggressive B-cell lymphoma with CNS involvement).
entries.finding: A CNS-directed regimen combining high-dose methotrexate/cytarabine/thiotepa with rituximab and systemic salvage produced meaningful responses in secondary CNS lymphoma. It grounds using CNS-penetrant systemic therapy (not intrathecal-only) for CNS involvement.
entries.limits: Single-arm, selected patients; intensive regimen not tolerable for all.

entries.population: Primary CNS lymphoma (methotrexate/cytarabine/thiotepa/rituximab backbone).
entries.finding: The methotrexate-cytarabine-thiotepa-rituximab backbone improved outcomes in CNS lymphoma and is the canonical CNS-penetrant systemic regimen. It grounds the requirement for a CNS-active systemic backbone, since these agents (unlike R-CHOP components) reach cytotoxic CNS levels.
entries.limits: Primary-CNS-lymphoma population; extrapolation to secondary CNS lymphoma is by mechanism/practice.

entries.population: Chemotherapy-sensitive relapsed aggressive non-Hodgkin lymphoma.
entries.finding: Autologous transplant improved survival over salvage chemotherapy only in patients whose relapse was chemotherapy-sensitive. This is the basis for requiring chemosensitivity before ASCT and for removing ASCT from the differential in chemorefractory disease.
entries.limits: Historic (pre-rituximab, pre-CAR-T) trial; establishes the chemosensitivity prerequisite, not modern regimen ranking.

entries.population: Adults with R/R LBCL after exactly one prior line who were NOT intended for HSCT (transplant-ineligible by objective criteria: age >=70, ECOG 2, or organ dysfunction).
entries.finding: In transplant-ineligible patients (median age 74, many ECOG 2 or with comorbidity), single-arm second-line lisocabtagene maraleucel produced an ORR of 80% and CR of 54% with low severe toxicity (grade 3 CRS 2%, grade 3 neurologic events 5%). It supports liso-cel as a 2L option for transplant-ineligible patients regardless of relapse timing, including late relapse.
entries.limits: Single-arm, no comparator; synthetic real-world control for the response threshold; older/comorbid by design.

entries.population: Transplant-ineligible R/R DLBCL (randomized vs BR, plus expansion); approved 3L+.
entries.finding: Polatuzumab vedotin added to bendamustine-rituximab improved outcomes over BR in transplant-ineligible R/R DLBCL and is approved from the third line. Two sequencing hazards matter: the bendamustine backbone is T-cell depleting and can compromise later apheresis and cellular-therapy manufacturing, and polatuzumab's MMAE payload adds to prior vincristine neuropathy.
entries.limits: Small randomized cohort; approved 3L+; bendamustine backbone is a sequencing hazard before planned cellular therapy.

entries.population: DLBCL patients aged >=80 receiving first-line therapy.
entries.finding: Attenuated-dose R-miniCHOP is a validated, evidence-based first-line regimen for DLBCL patients aged 80 and older, achieving meaningful cure rates with acceptable toxicity. It grounds that age-attenuated first-line therapy is appropriate by design, so progression shortly after it reflects refractory biology rather than under-dosing.
entries.limits: First-line, elderly-specific single-arm; does not address relapsed/refractory choice.

entries.population: Refractory DLBCL (refractory to chemoimmunotherapy or relapse <=12 months post-ASCT), pooled international cohort.
entries.finding: Refractory DLBCL in the pre-CAR-T era had a poor prognosis, with an ORR to next therapy around 26% and median overall survival of roughly 6 months. This benchmark frames refractoriness as the dominant adverse driver and argues against under-treating a fit patient with refractory disease.
entries.limits: Retrospective/pooled, pre-CAR-T era; establishes prognosis, not a specific regimen choice.

entries.population: Transplant-ineligible R/R DLBCL after >=1 prior line; glofitamab + GemOx vs R-GemOx.
entries.finding: Glofitamab plus GemOx improved outcomes versus R-GemOx, but the US regulator issued a Complete Response Letter (2025) citing insufficient applicability of the Asia-weighted enrollment to the US population, so it is not FDA-approved. Glofitamab is US-approved only as monotherapy at 3rd line or later.
entries.limits: Not FDA-approved in the US (CRL); enrollment weighted outside the US; combination and 2L use are off-label in the US.

entries.population: ASCT-ineligible R/R large B-cell lymphoma, second line and later.
entries.finding: In transplant-ineligible R/R LBCL, mosunetuzumab plus polatuzumab vedotin tripled progression-free survival versus R-GemOx (median 11.5 vs 3.8 months, HR 0.41) with mostly low-grade CRS. It is an evidence-supported, chemo-light, largely outpatient option, but its efficacy can under-treat a phenotypically fit patient with bulky, primary-refractory disease.
entries.limits: Comparator was R-GemOx, not CAR-T; positioned for transplant-ineligible patients; polatuzumab carries bendamustine-independent neuropathy risk.

entries.population: Adults with R/R LBCL (>=2 prior lines), a cohort that permitted secondary CNS involvement.
entries.finding: The pivotal liso-cel study allowed patients with secondary CNS lymphoma and reported feasibility without excess neurologic toxicity in that subgroup. It, plus liso-cel's lower ICANS profile, supports liso-cel as a reasonable CD19 CAR-T choice when baseline CNS disease is present.
entries.limits: CNS subgroup small and non-randomized; CNS involvement is not a routine on-label CAR-T indication.

entries.population: Adults with LBCL primary-refractory to or relapsed within 12 months of first-line therapy, eligible for autologous transplant.
entries.finding: Second-line lisocabtagene maraleucel improved event-free survival versus standard salvage chemoimmunotherapy plus autologous transplant in transplant-eligible patients with early treatment failure. Together with ZUMA-7 it establishes 2L CD19 CAR-T as superior to salvage/ASCT for primary-refractory or early-relapse LBCL.
entries.limits: Transplant-eligible, early-failure population; not the transplant-ineligible or late-relapse setting.

entries.population: Classification of mature B-cell neoplasms.
entries.finding: The classification defines high-grade B-cell lymphoma with MYC and BCL2 rearrangements ('double-hit') as requiring MYC and BCL2 translocations detected by FISH. MYC amplification (copy-number gain) is not the same entity and is 'double-hit-like' with less-established prognostic weight.
entries.anchor_quote: requires MYC and BCL2 translocations by FISH
entries.limits: Classification/consensus, not outcome data; prognostic weight of amplification vs rearrangement differs.

entries.population: Transplant-INTENDED adults with LBCL that was primary-refractory to, or relapsed within 12 months of, first-line chemoimmunotherapy.
entries.finding: In transplant-intended patients with early treatment failure (primary-refractory or relapse within 12 months), second-line axicabtagene ciloleucel improved event-free survival over salvage chemoimmunotherapy followed by autologous transplant (median EFS 8.3 vs 2.0 months, HR 0.40), and a later prespecified analysis showed an overall-survival benefit. This is the randomized basis for using 2L CAR-T rather than salvage/ASCT in the early-failure population.
entries.limits: Transplant-INTENDED population only; does not speak to transplant-ineligible or late-relapse patients. Grade >=3 neurologic events 21%.
